name: Introduction to Economics
description: An accessible introduction to fundamental economic principles covering scarcity, supply and demand, market structures, national income, and the roles of money and government in the economy.

modules.title: Scarcity & Economic Thinking
modules.objectives: Define scarcity and explain why it is the central economic problem; distinguish between wants and needs; explain opportunity cost and the role of trade-offs in economic decision-making.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 1 — What Is Economics?
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Scarcity, Choice, and Opportunity Cost
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Economics Practice
modules.assignment: List three personal decisions you made this week and identify the opportunity cost of each; explain how scarcity forced you to make a trade-off in each case.

modules.title: Supply & Demand Basics
modules.objectives: State the law of demand and the law of supply; list the determinants of supply and demand; draw supply and demand curves and identify factors that shift each curve.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 3 — Demand, Supply, and Equilibrium
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Supply and Demand: How Markets Work
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Demand and Supply Practice
modules.assignment: Draw a supply curve and a demand curve for a product of your choice; label equilibrium price and quantity, then show and explain the effect of one shift in supply and one shift in demand.

modules.title: Market Equilibrium & Price
modules.objectives: Identify market equilibrium as the price where quantity supplied equals quantity demanded; predict the effects of supply and demand shifts on equilibrium price and quantity; explain surpluses and shortages.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 3.3 — Changes in Equilibrium
modules.assignment: Using a supply-demand diagram, predict and illustrate what happens to equilibrium price and quantity when consumer incomes rise for a normal good and input costs rise simultaneously.

modules.title: Elasticity
modules.objectives: Define and calculate price elasticity of demand and supply; distinguish elastic from inelastic demand; explain how elasticity affects total revenue and the impact of taxes.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 5 — Elasticity
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Price Elasticity of Demand
modules.assignment: Given quantity and price data, calculate the price elasticity of demand using the midpoint method; determine whether the good is elastic or inelastic and what a price increase would do to total revenue.

modules.title: Consumer Choice & Utility
modules.objectives: Define utility and marginal utility; apply the law of diminishing marginal utility; explain how consumers maximize utility by allocating spending across goods.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 6.1 — Consumption Choices
modules.assignment: Complete a utility table for consuming different quantities of a snack; calculate marginal utility and identify the point of diminishing returns; determine how you would allocate $10 between two goods to maximize total utility.

modules.title: Production & Costs
modules.objectives: Distinguish fixed from variable costs and short-run from long-run production; calculate total cost, average total cost, and marginal cost; explain the relationship between production and costs.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 7 — Cost and Industry Structure
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Fixed and Variable Costs
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Average and Marginal Costs Explained
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Production and Costs Practice
modules.assignment: Given a table of output and variable costs with fixed costs of $200, calculate total cost, average total cost, average variable cost, and marginal cost for each output level; graph ATC and MC.

modules.title: Types of Market Structures
modules.objectives: Identify and compare the four main market structures — perfect competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, and monopoly — using characteristics such as number of sellers, product type, and barriers to entry.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 8–10 — Market Structures
modules.assignment: Create a comparison chart of all four market structures across at least six characteristics; identify a real-world example of each market structure and justify your classification.

modules.title: Labor Markets
modules.objectives: Explain how wages are determined by supply and demand in labor markets; analyze the effects of minimum wage laws; describe the role of unions and human capital in wage differences.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 14 — Labor Markets
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Minimum Wage: Effects on Employment
modules.assignment: Draw a labor market diagram showing equilibrium wage and employment; add a minimum wage set above equilibrium and show the resulting surplus of labor (unemployment).

modules.title: National Income & GDP
modules.objectives: Define GDP and explain its importance as an economic indicator; calculate GDP using the expenditure approach; distinguish between GDP and GNP; understand limitations of GDP as a welfare measure.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 19 — Measuring GDP
modules.video2Note: YouTube — What Is GDP and Why Does It Matter?
modules.assignment: Using a provided set of national data (consumption, investment, government spending, exports, imports), calculate GDP using the expenditure approach; explain one reason GDP might overstate and one reason it might understate actual wellbeing.

modules.title: Unemployment
modules.objectives: Define unemployment and explain how the unemployment rate is calculated; distinguish frictional, structural, cyclical, and seasonal unemployment; understand the concept of the natural rate of unemployment.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 21 — Unemployment
modules.assignment: Given data on the population, labor force, and number unemployed, calculate the unemployment rate and labor force participation rate; categorize five given scenarios as frictional, structural, or cyclical unemployment.

modules.title: Inflation & Price Level
modules.objectives: Define inflation and explain how the Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures it; distinguish demand-pull from cost-push inflation; analyze the effects of inflation on consumers, savers, and debtors.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 22 — Inflation
modules.assignment: Using a simple market basket of three goods with prices in two years, calculate the CPI and the inflation rate; identify one winner and one loser from unexpected inflation and explain why.

modules.title: Money, Banking & the Fed
modules.objectives: Define money and its three functions; explain how banks create money through the deposit-expansion process; describe the Federal Reserve's structure and its three main monetary policy tools.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 27–28 — Money and the Federal Reserve
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Money and the Federal Reserve
modules.assignment: Explain the three functions of money using examples; trace how a $1,000 deposit with a 10% reserve requirement creates money through the banking system and calculate the maximum expansion.

modules.title: Government's Role in the Economy
modules.objectives: Describe the government's roles as provider, regulator, and stabilizer; understand fiscal policy tools; analyze the trade-offs of government intervention including taxes, spending, and redistribution.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 30 — Government Budgets and Fiscal Policy
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Role of Government in a Market Economy
modules.assignment: Research the current US federal budget: identify the three largest spending categories and the main sources of revenue; write a paragraph arguing whether the government's current level of involvement in the economy is appropriate.

modules.title: International Trade & Globalization
modules.objectives: Explain comparative advantage and the gains from trade; describe common trade barriers (tariffs, quotas); identify winners and losers from international trade; evaluate the pros and cons of globalization.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 33 — International Trade
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — International Trade and Comparative Advantage
modules.assignment: Given simple production data for two countries, calculate comparative advantage and determine a mutually beneficial trade; list two arguments for and two arguments against free trade.

quizQuestions.question: Scarcity in economics means that:
options: Resources are limited relative to unlimited wants
quizQuestions.answer: Resources are limited relative to unlimited wants
quizQuestions.explanation: Scarcity is the fundamental economic problem: human wants are unlimited while the resources to satisfy them are finite, forcing individuals and societies to make choices.

quizQuestions.question: Opportunity cost is best defined as:
options: The monetary price of a good
The value of the next best alternative foregone
The cost of producing one additional unit
The total cost of all resources used
quizQuestions.answer: The value of the next best alternative foregone
quizQuestions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of what you give up when you make a choice — the best alternative you did not choose.

quizQuestions.question: The production possibilities frontier (PPF) illustrates:
options: The maximum combinations of two goods a society can produce given resources
The relationship between supply and demand
quizQuestions.answer: The maximum combinations of two goods a society can produce given resources
quizQuestions.explanation: The PPF shows the trade-off between producing two goods when all resources are used efficiently; points on the curve are efficient, inside are inefficient, outside are unattainable.

quizQuestions.question: If you choose to study instead of watching a movie, the opportunity cost of studying is:
options: The enjoyment you would have gotten from the movie
The grade you receive on the exam
The tuition you pay for the course
quizQuestions.answer: The enjoyment you would have gotten from the movie
quizQuestions.explanation: The opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative foregone — in this case, the enjoyment of the movie you did not watch.

quizQuestions.question: Economics is best described as the study of:
options: How people make choices under scarcity
How to make money in the stock market
quizQuestions.answer: How people make choices under scarcity
quizQuestions.explanation: Economics studies how individuals, businesses, and governments make decisions to allocate limited resources to satisfy unlimited wants.

quizQuestions.question: The law of demand states that, all else equal:
options: As price rises, quantity demanded rises
As price falls, quantity demanded falls
As price rises, quantity demanded falls
quizQuestions.answer: As price rises, quantity demanded falls
quizQuestions.explanation: The law of demand reflects the inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded — consumers buy less of a good as its price increases.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following would SHIFT the demand curve for pizza to the RIGHT?
options: A decrease in the price of pizza
An increase in consumer income (pizza is a normal good)
An increase in the price of a pizza ingredient
A decrease in the number of pizza restaurants
quizQuestions.answer: An increase in consumer income (pizza is a normal good)
quizQuestions.explanation: Higher income increases demand for normal goods, shifting the demand curve right (more demanded at every price). A price change causes movement along the curve, not a shift.

quizQuestions.question: The law of supply states that, all else equal:
options: As price rises, quantity supplied falls
As price falls, quantity supplied rises
As price rises, quantity supplied rises
Supply is not affected by price
quizQuestions.answer: As price rises, quantity supplied rises
quizQuestions.explanation: Producers are willing to supply more of a good at higher prices because higher prices make production more profitable.

quizQuestions.question: If the price of steel (an input) rises, what happens to the supply of cars?
quizQuestions.answer: Supply decreases (shifts left)
quizQuestions.explanation: Higher input costs increase production costs, making it less profitable to produce cars at any given price, decreasing supply.

quizQuestions.question: A complement to coffee is:
quizQuestions.answer: Coffee creamer
quizQuestions.explanation: Complements are goods typically consumed together. Coffee and creamer are complements; if the price of coffee rises, demand for creamer falls.

quizQuestions.question: Market equilibrium occurs when:
options: The government sets a fair price
quizQuestions.answer: Quantity supplied equals quantity demanded
quizQuestions.explanation: Equilibrium is the price at which the market 'clears' — the amount sellers want to sell exactly equals the amount buyers want to buy.

quizQuestions.question: A surplus in a market means:
options: Quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the current price
Quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded at the current price
quizQuestions.answer: Quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded at the current price
quizQuestions.explanation: A surplus (excess supply) occurs when the price is above equilibrium — producers want to sell more than buyers want to buy, putting downward pressure on price.

quizQuestions.question: If both supply and demand increase simultaneously, what definitely happens to equilibrium quantity?
options: It depends on the size of each shift
quizQuestions.answer: It rises
quizQuestions.explanation: Both a supply increase and a demand increase put upward pressure on quantity. Equilibrium quantity definitely rises; the effect on price is ambiguous.

quizQuestions.question: A price floor set above equilibrium will create:
quizQuestions.answer: A surplus
quizQuestions.explanation: A price floor above equilibrium (e.g., minimum wage) keeps prices artificially high, so quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded — a surplus.

quizQuestions.question: When demand increases and supply remains unchanged, equilibrium price will:
quizQuestions.answer: Rise
quizQuestions.explanation: An increase in demand (rightward shift) with unchanged supply creates excess demand at the old price, pushing the equilibrium price upward.

quizQuestions.question: If a 10% rise in price leads to a 20% fall in quantity demanded, price elasticity of demand is:
quizQuestions.answer: 2.0 — elastic
quizQuestions.explanation: PED = % change in Qd / % change in P = 20% / 10% = 2.0. Since PED > 1, demand is elastic — consumers are very responsive to price changes.

quizQuestions.question: Goods with few substitutes tend to have:
quizQuestions.answer: Inelastic demand
quizQuestions.explanation: With few substitutes, consumers cannot easily switch away when price rises, making demand less responsive to price — inelastic demand.

quizQuestions.question: If demand is inelastic and price rises, total revenue will:
quizQuestions.answer: Increase
quizQuestions.explanation: With inelastic demand, the percentage decrease in quantity demanded is smaller than the percentage price increase, so price × quantity (total revenue) rises.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following goods is likely to have the MOST elastic demand?
options: A specific luxury sports car brand
quizQuestions.answer: A specific luxury sports car brand
quizQuestions.explanation: Luxury goods with many substitutes (other car brands or models) tend to have elastic demand — a price increase causes a large drop in quantity demanded.

quizQuestions.question: Cross-price elasticity of demand measures:
options: How quantity demanded of one good responds to a change in income
How quantity demanded of one good responds to a price change of another good
How producers respond to a change in input prices
The price sensitivity of a firm's product
quizQuestions.answer: How quantity demanded of one good responds to a price change of another good
quizQuestions.explanation: Cross-price elasticity = % change in Qd of Good A / % change in price of Good B. Positive values indicate substitutes; negative values indicate complements.

quizQuestions.question: Marginal utility is the:
options: Total satisfaction from consuming a good
Additional satisfaction from consuming one more unit
Change in price per unit consumed
quizQuestions.answer: Additional satisfaction from consuming one more unit
quizQuestions.explanation: Marginal utility is the extra satisfaction (utility) gained from consuming one additional unit of a good or service.

quizQuestions.question: The law of diminishing marginal utility states that:
options: Total utility eventually decreases with consumption
Marginal utility increases as more units are consumed
As more units are consumed, each additional unit provides less additional satisfaction
quizQuestions.answer: As more units are consumed, each additional unit provides less additional satisfaction
quizQuestions.explanation: After a point, each additional unit of a good adds less to total utility than the previous unit did — the classic pattern of diminishing marginal utility.

quizQuestions.question: A consumer maximizes utility when:
options: Marginal utility of every good equals zero
The ratio of marginal utility to price is equal for all goods purchased
Marginal utility of every good is equal
quizQuestions.answer: The ratio of marginal utility to price is equal for all goods purchased
quizQuestions.explanation: Utility is maximized when MU_A/P_A = MU_B/P_B for all goods — the consumer gets the same bang per buck from every good purchased.

quizQuestions.question: If the price of Good A rises, a utility-maximizing consumer should:
options: Buy more of Good A until MU rises
Buy less of Good A and possibly more of substitutes
Increase spending on all goods equally
quizQuestions.answer: Buy less of Good A and possibly more of substitutes
quizQuestions.explanation: A price increase lowers the MU/P ratio for Good A, so the consumer reduces purchases of A (until MU/P rises again) and shifts to other goods.

quizQuestions.question: Consumer surplus is best described as:
options: The amount consumers pay for a good
The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay
The tax revenue collected from consumers
quizQuestions.answer: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay
quizQuestions.explanation: Consumer surplus is the benefit consumers receive by paying less than their maximum willingness to pay — the area below the demand curve and above market price.

quizQuestions.question: Fixed costs are costs that:
options: Do not change with output in the short run
Depend on the number of workers hired
quizQuestions.answer: Do not change with output in the short run
quizQuestions.explanation: Fixed costs (rent, insurance, equipment) must be paid regardless of how much is produced; they cannot be avoided in the short run.

quizQuestions.question: Marginal cost is the:
options: Cost of producing one more unit of output
quizQuestions.answer: Cost of producing one more unit of output
quizQuestions.explanation: Marginal cost = change in total cost / change in quantity. It measures the additional cost of producing one more unit.

quizQuestions.question: If a firm has total costs of $500 at 10 units and $550 at 11 units, the marginal cost of the 11th unit is:
quizQuestions.explanation: MC = change in total cost / change in quantity = ($550 − $500) / (11 − 10) = $50.

quizQuestions.question: Average total cost (ATC) is calculated as:
quizQuestions.answer: Total cost / quantity
quizQuestions.explanation: ATC = TC / Q. It measures the per-unit cost of production, including both fixed and variable costs.

quizQuestions.question: In the short run, a firm CANNOT change its:
quizQuestions.answer: Fixed inputs
quizQuestions.explanation: In the short run, at least one input (such as factory size or equipment) is fixed and cannot be adjusted. Only variable inputs can be changed.

quizQuestions.question: Which market structure has the MOST competition?
quizQuestions.answer: Perfect competition
quizQuestions.explanation: Perfect competition has the most competition: many sellers, identical products, free entry/exit, and perfect information — no single firm has pricing power.

quizQuestions.question: A monopoly differs from perfect competition primarily because the monopolist:
options: Is the sole seller and faces no close substitutes
Sets price equal to marginal cost
quizQuestions.answer: Is the sole seller and faces no close substitutes
quizQuestions.explanation: A monopoly is defined by a single seller with significant barriers to entry and no close substitute for its product, giving it substantial pricing power.

quizQuestions.question: An example of an oligopoly in the United States is the:
quizQuestions.answer: US airline industry
quizQuestions.explanation: The US airline industry is dominated by a few large carriers (Delta, United, American, Southwest), making it an oligopoly with high barriers to entry.

quizQuestions.question: Product differentiation in monopolistic competition means that:
options: Each firm's product is slightly different from competitors' products
There is only one product in the market
Products are regulated by the government
quizQuestions.answer: Each firm's product is slightly different from competitors' products
quizQuestions.explanation: In monopolistic competition, firms differentiate their products through quality, features, branding, or location — giving each some pricing power.

quizQuestions.question: Barriers to entry in a monopoly market might include:
options: Government licenses, patents, or control of key resources
quizQuestions.answer: Government licenses, patents, or control of key resources
quizQuestions.explanation: Barriers to entry such as patents, government regulations, control of essential inputs, or high startup costs prevent new competitors from entering the market.

quizQuestions.question: In a competitive labor market, the equilibrium wage is determined by:
options: The intersection of labor supply and labor demand
quizQuestions.answer: The intersection of labor supply and labor demand
quizQuestions.explanation: Like any competitive market, wages in a competitive labor market are determined by supply and demand forces.

quizQuestions.question: A minimum wage set above the market equilibrium wage will most likely:
quizQuestions.answer: Decrease employment
quizQuestions.explanation: A minimum wage above equilibrium creates a surplus of labor (more workers want jobs than employers want to hire), reducing employment below the free-market level.

quizQuestions.question: Human capital refers to:
options: The knowledge and skills workers acquire through education and training
quizQuestions.answer: The knowledge and skills workers acquire through education and training
quizQuestions.explanation: Human capital is the productive capacity embodied in workers through education, on-the-job training, and experience.

quizQuestions.question: If labor demand increases (shifts right) and labor supply stays the same, wages will:
quizQuestions.answer: Rise
quizQuestions.explanation: Increased demand for labor with unchanged supply creates upward pressure on wages, raising the equilibrium wage.

quizQuestions.question: Labor unions generally aim to:
options: Lower wages to keep firms competitive
Increase wages and improve working conditions for their members
Reduce the number of workers in an industry
quizQuestions.answer: Increase wages and improve working conditions for their members
quizQuestions.explanation: Labor unions collectively bargain on behalf of workers to negotiate higher wages, better benefits, and improved workplace conditions.

quizQuestions.question: GDP stands for:
quizQuestions.answer: Gross Domestic Product
quizQuestions.explanation: GDP — Gross Domestic Product — measures the total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given period.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is included in GDP?
options: Government purchase of a new police car
quizQuestions.answer: Government purchase of a new police car
quizQuestions.explanation: Government spending on new goods and services is part of GDP (the G component). Used goods, financial transactions, and unpaid household work are excluded.

quizQuestions.question: The expenditure approach to GDP is: GDP =
quizQuestions.answer: C + I + G + (X − M)
quizQuestions.explanation: The expenditure approach adds Consumption (C), Investment (I), Government spending (G), and Net Exports (X − M) to calculate GDP.

quizQuestions.question: Real GDP differs from nominal GDP in that real GDP:
options: Adjusts for changes in the price level
Is always larger than nominal GDP
quizQuestions.answer: Adjusts for changes in the price level
quizQuestions.explanation: Real GDP removes the effect of price changes (inflation), allowing comparison of actual output across different years.

quizQuestions.question: One major limitation of GDP as a measure of well-being is that it:
options: Measures too many goods and services
Ignores income distribution and non-market activities
Is too difficult to calculate accurately
quizQuestions.answer: Ignores income distribution and non-market activities
quizQuestions.explanation: GDP does not account for how income is distributed, unpaid work (household labor, volunteering), environmental quality, or leisure time.

quizQuestions.question: A person who recently lost their job and is actively searching for a new one is classified as:
quizQuestions.answer: Unemployed
quizQuestions.explanation: A person is counted as unemployed if they are jobless, available to work, and actively seeking employment.

quizQuestions.question: Frictional unemployment occurs when workers are:
options: Temporarily between jobs or searching for better positions
Unable to find work due to lack of skills
quizQuestions.answer: Temporarily between jobs or searching for better positions
quizQuestions.explanation: Frictional unemployment is short-term and voluntary, occurring as workers transition between jobs — a normal feature of a healthy labor market.

quizQuestions.question: Structural unemployment results from:
options: A mismatch between worker skills and job requirements due to industry changes
quizQuestions.answer: A mismatch between worker skills and job requirements due to industry changes
quizQuestions.explanation: Structural unemployment occurs when technological change or industry shifts make certain skills obsolete, requiring workers to retrain for different occupations.

quizQuestions.question: Cyclical unemployment is caused by:
options: A decline in aggregate demand during a recession
A permanent shift in industry structure
quizQuestions.answer: A decline in aggregate demand during a recession
quizQuestions.explanation: Cyclical unemployment rises during economic downturns (recessions) as businesses cut production and lay off workers due to decreased demand.

quizQuestions.question: The natural rate of unemployment is the rate that:
options: Is always zero in a healthy economy
Includes only frictional and structural unemployment
Is equal to the cyclical unemployment rate
quizQuestions.answer: Includes only frictional and structural unemployment
quizQuestions.explanation: The natural rate is the unemployment rate consistent with full employment — it includes frictional and structural unemployment but not cyclical unemployment.

quizQuestions.question: Inflation is defined as:
options: An increase in the money supply
A sustained increase in the general price level over time
A rise in the unemployment rate
quizQuestions.answer: A sustained increase in the general price level over time
quizQuestions.explanation: Inflation is the rate at which the general level of prices for goods and services rises, eroding purchasing power over time.

quizQuestions.question: The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures:
options: The total output of the economy
Changes in the price of a fixed basket of goods and services bought by a typical household
The government's spending on consumer goods
quizQuestions.answer: Changes in the price of a fixed basket of goods and services bought by a typical household
quizQuestions.explanation: The CPI tracks how the cost of a representative basket of consumer goods and services changes over time, measuring the inflation experienced by typical households.

quizQuestions.question: Demand-pull inflation occurs when:
options: Aggregate demand exceeds the economy's productive capacity
quizQuestions.answer: Aggregate demand exceeds the economy's productive capacity
quizQuestions.explanation: Demand-pull inflation — 'too much money chasing too few goods' — happens when aggregate demand grows faster than aggregate supply.

quizQuestions.question: Who benefits from unexpected inflation?
quizQuestions.answer: Borrowers with fixed-rate loans
quizQuestions.explanation: Unexpected inflation erodes the real value of debt, benefiting borrowers who repay loans in dollars worth less than when they borrowed.

quizQuestions.question: If the CPI was 200 in Year 1 and 210 in Year 2, the inflation rate was:
quizQuestions.explanation: Inflation rate = (CPI Year 2 − CPI Year 1) / CPI Year 1 × 100 = (210 − 200) / 200 × 100 = 5%.

quizQuestions.question: The three functions of money are:
options: Medium of exchange, unit of account, and store of value
quizQuestions.answer: Medium of exchange, unit of account, and store of value
quizQuestions.explanation: Money functions as a medium of exchange (used to buy goods), a unit of account (measuring prices), and a store of value (preserving purchasing power over time).

quizQuestions.question: When a bank receives a $1,000 deposit and the reserve requirement is 10%, it can lend out:
quizQuestions.explanation: The bank must hold $100 in reserves (10% × $1,000) and can lend out the remaining $900.

quizQuestions.question: The Federal Reserve was created to:
options: Serve as the central bank and manage the US monetary system
quizQuestions.answer: Serve as the central bank and manage the US monetary system
quizQuestions.explanation: The Fed was established in 1913 to provide a stable monetary and financial system, acting as a lender of last resort and controlling the money supply.

quizQuestions.question: When the Fed SELLS government bonds, the money supply:
quizQuestions.answer: Decreases
quizQuestions.explanation: When the Fed sells bonds, buyers pay with money, reducing bank reserves and the money supply — a contractionary monetary policy action.

quizQuestions.question: M1 money supply includes:
options: Currency in circulation and checking account deposits
quizQuestions.answer: Currency in circulation and checking account deposits
quizQuestions.explanation: M1 is the narrowest measure of money supply, including the most liquid assets: physical currency and demand deposits (checking accounts).

quizQuestions.question: A public good is one that is:
quizQuestions.answer: Non-excludable and non-rival
quizQuestions.explanation: Public goods cannot exclude non-payers (non-excludable) and one person's use does not reduce availability to others (non-rival). Examples: national defense, lighthouses.

quizQuestions.question: A negative externality leads to market:
options: Overproduction relative to the social optimum
quizQuestions.answer: Overproduction relative to the social optimum
quizQuestions.explanation: When producers do not bear the full social cost of their actions (e.g., pollution), they overproduce — more than is socially optimal.

quizQuestions.question: The primary purpose of the federal income tax is to:
options: Raise revenue to fund government spending
quizQuestions.answer: Raise revenue to fund government spending
quizQuestions.explanation: The main purpose of taxation is to finance government expenditures on public goods, services, and transfer programs.

quizQuestions.question: A progressive tax system means that:
options: Everyone pays the same dollar amount
Higher income earners pay a higher percentage of their income in taxes
Tax rates decrease as income rises
quizQuestions.answer: Higher income earners pay a higher percentage of their income in taxes
quizQuestions.explanation: In a progressive tax system (like the US federal income tax), the marginal tax rate rises with income — higher earners pay a larger share.

quizQuestions.question: When government spending exceeds tax revenues, the result is a:
quizQuestions.answer: Budget deficit
quizQuestions.explanation: A budget deficit occurs when government expenditures exceed tax revenues in a given year, requiring the government to borrow to cover the shortfall.

quizQuestions.question: Comparative advantage means a country should produce goods for which it has:
quizQuestions.answer: The lowest opportunity cost
quizQuestions.explanation: Comparative advantage is based on opportunity cost — a country specializes in goods it can produce at the lowest opportunity cost relative to trading partners.

quizQuestions.question: A tariff is a tax placed on:
quizQuestions.answer: Imported goods
quizQuestions.explanation: A tariff is a tax imposed by a government on imported goods, raising their price and making domestic goods more competitive.

quizQuestions.question: Free trade is generally argued to benefit countries because it:
options: Allows specialization and gains from comparative advantage
Always results in a trade surplus
quizQuestions.answer: Allows specialization and gains from comparative advantage
quizQuestions.explanation: Free trade enables countries to specialize in what they produce most efficiently and trade for other goods, expanding total consumption possibilities.

quizQuestions.question: Globalization refers to:
options: Countries closing their borders to trade
The increasing integration of economies, cultures, and people worldwide
quizQuestions.answer: The increasing integration of economies, cultures, and people worldwide
quizQuestions.explanation: Globalization describes the process by which economies, markets, cultures, and people become more interconnected through trade, investment, and communication.

quizQuestions.question: One argument AGAINST free trade is that it can:
options: Lead to job losses in industries that cannot compete with cheaper imports
quizQuestions.answer: Lead to job losses in industries that cannot compete with cheaper imports
quizQuestions.explanation: While free trade generates net benefits, it can displace workers in import-competing industries — a significant distributional concern.

questions.question: The production possibilities frontier bows outward (is concave) because of:
options: Increasing opportunity costs as production is shifted
questions.answer: Increasing opportunity costs as production is shifted
questions.explanation: Resources are not equally suited to all uses; as you produce more of one good, you must use increasingly less suitable resources, raising opportunity costs.

questions.question: If the price of a substitute good rises, what happens to the demand for the original good?
questions.explanation: When the price of a substitute rises, consumers switch to the original good, increasing its demand (shifting right).

questions.question: A price ceiling set BELOW equilibrium price will cause:
questions.explanation: A binding price ceiling keeps prices artificially low, increasing quantity demanded while decreasing quantity supplied — creating a shortage.

questions.question: Using the midpoint method, if price rises from $10 to $20 and quantity demanded falls from 100 to 80, PED =
Total utility is maximized at the point where:
questions.explanation: Total utility reaches its maximum when marginal utility is zero. Consuming beyond this point causes marginal utility to become negative, reducing total utility.

questions.question: Which cost is always declining as output increases?
questions.explanation: Average fixed cost (AFC) = fixed costs / quantity. Since fixed costs are constant, AFC always falls as output rises — spreading fixed costs over more units.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of BOTH monopolistic competition and perfect competition?
questions.explanation: Both market structures have many sellers and free entry/exit. They differ in that monopolistic competition has differentiated products while perfect competition has identical products.

questions.question: Which of the following is a shift factor for SUPPLY (not a movement along supply)?
options: A change in the product's own price
Improved technology that lowers production costs
questions.answer: Improved technology that lowers production costs
questions.explanation: Technology improvements lower production costs and shift the supply curve right. A change in the good's own price causes movement along the existing supply curve.

questions.question: Structural unemployment is best addressed by:
questions.explanation: Structural unemployment results from skills mismatches; retraining workers for in-demand occupations directly addresses the root cause.

questions.question: A firm in a perfectly competitive market earns zero economic profit in the long run because:
options: Entry of new firms eliminates profit by lowering price to minimum ATC
The firm has no marginal revenue
Product prices are set by unions
questions.answer: Entry of new firms eliminates profit by lowering price to minimum ATC
questions.explanation: Positive profits attract new entrants, increasing supply and driving price down until economic profit equals zero in the long run.

questions.question: If demand is elastic and price decreases, total revenue will:
questions.explanation: With elastic demand (PED > 1), a price decrease causes a proportionally larger increase in quantity demanded, raising total revenue.

questions.question: The key characteristic distinguishing oligopoly from other market structures is:
options: Strategic interdependence among a few dominant firms
A single seller with complete market control
questions.answer: Strategic interdependence among a few dominant firms
questions.explanation: Oligopoly firms must consider rivals' reactions to their pricing/output decisions — this strategic interdependence is the defining feature.

questions.question: Which of the following would DECREASE labor demand in a particular industry?
options: An increase in demand for the industry's product
A technological innovation that replaces workers with machines
An increase in human capital investment
questions.answer: A technological innovation that replaces workers with machines
questions.explanation: Labor-saving technology reduces the quantity of labor needed to produce each unit, shifting the labor demand curve left.

questions.question: Producer surplus is the area:
options: Below demand curve and above price
Above supply curve and below equilibrium price
Between supply and demand at disequilibrium quantities
Equal to fixed costs in the long run
questions.answer: Above supply curve and below equilibrium price
questions.explanation: Producer surplus is the benefit producers receive by selling at the market price above their minimum acceptable price (supply curve) — the area above supply and below price.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four factors of production?
questions.explanation: The four factors of production are land, labor, capital (physical tools and machinery), and entrepreneurship. Currency is a financial medium, not a productive resource.

questions.question: Deflation is defined as:
options: A sustained decrease in the general price level
questions.answer: A sustained decrease in the general price level
questions.explanation: Deflation is the opposite of inflation — a sustained decrease in the general price level, which can be harmful because it delays purchases and increases the real burden of debt.

questions.question: When the Federal Reserve lowers the reserve requirement, the most direct effect is:
options: Increased money creation potential by banks
questions.answer: Increased money creation potential by banks
questions.explanation: A lower reserve requirement means banks must hold less in reserve and can lend more, increasing the money multiplier and expanding the money supply.

questions.question: A budget deficit occurs when:
options: The money supply grows too fast
questions.explanation: A budget deficit means the government spends more than it collects in taxes in a given year, requiring borrowing to fund the difference.

questions.question: If the US has a trade deficit, it means:
questions.explanation: A trade deficit (negative net exports) means the value of a country's imports exceeds the value of its exports.

questions.question: Which of the following would be considered expansionary monetary policy?
options: Selling government bonds in the open market
questions.explanation: Lowering the federal funds rate reduces the cost of borrowing, encouraging banks to lend more, increasing the money supply and stimulating aggregate demand.

questions.question: Which of the following would be most likely to cause cost-push inflation?
options: A sharp rise in oil prices
questions.answer: A sharp rise in oil prices
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation results from rising input costs (like oil) increasing production costs across the economy, shifting aggregate supply left.

questions.question: GDP is calculated using which approach in the formula C + I + G + (X − M)?
questions.explanation: The expenditure approach sums all spending on final goods and services: Consumption, Investment, Government spending, and Net Exports.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the free-rider problem?
options: People who benefit from public goods without paying for them
Firms that free-ride on government subsidies
questions.answer: People who benefit from public goods without paying for them
questions.explanation: Because public goods are non-excludable, people can consume them without contributing to their cost — undermining private provision.

questions.question: If Country A has a comparative advantage in producing cars, it means:
options: Country A produces cars using fewer total resources than any other country
Country A can produce cars at a lower opportunity cost than its trading partners
Country A has the most advanced car technology
Country A exports more cars than any other country
questions.answer: Country A can produce cars at a lower opportunity cost than its trading partners
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage is defined by relative opportunity cost, not absolute productivity — the basis for all gains from trade.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of structural unemployment?
options: A teacher laid off during a recession due to budget cuts
A coal miner who loses their job as coal plants switch to natural gas
A ski instructor unemployed in summer
A recent graduate searching for their first job
questions.answer: A coal miner who loses their job as coal plants switch to natural gas
questions.explanation: The coal miner is structurally unemployed because their skills no longer match industry needs due to a fundamental shift in the energy sector.

questions.question: Expansionary fiscal policy during a recession would include:
options: Cutting taxes and increasing government spending
questions.answer: Cutting taxes and increasing government spending
questions.explanation: Expansionary fiscal policy boosts aggregate demand by putting more money in consumers' hands (tax cuts) and directly increasing government spending.

questions.question: The money multiplier equals 1 / reserve requirement. If the reserve requirement is 0.25, the money multiplier is:
questions.explanation: Money multiplier = 1 / 0.25 = 4. Each dollar in new reserves can support $4 in total deposits.

questions.question: A quota differs from a tariff in that a quota:
options: Generates tax revenue for the government
Sets a physical limit on the quantity of imports
questions.answer: Sets a physical limit on the quantity of imports
questions.explanation: Unlike a tariff (which is a tax), a quota directly limits the volume of imports allowed. Both raise domestic prices, but only tariffs generate government revenue.

questions.question: Stagflation is a condition characterized by:
options: High unemployment and high inflation simultaneously
Rapid GDP growth with falling prices
questions.answer: High unemployment and high inflation simultaneously
questions.explanation: Stagflation — a combination of stagnation (high unemployment, slow growth) and inflation — is associated with negative supply shocks like the 1970s oil crisis.

questions.question: When the price level rises, the purchasing power of money:
questions.explanation: Inflation reduces purchasing power — each dollar buys fewer goods and services when the general price level is higher.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of a public good?
options: A national park with an entrance fee
questions.explanation: National defense is non-excludable (everyone in the country is protected) and non-rival (one person's protection doesn't reduce another's), making it a classic public good.

questions.question: Which Federal Reserve tool is used MOST frequently to conduct monetary policy?
questions.explanation: Open market operations — the purchase and sale of US Treasury securities — are the Fed's primary and most flexible monetary policy tool.

questions.question: In a competitive labor market, if the demand for a product rises sharply, what happens to wages in that industry?
options: Wages fall because more workers are needed
Wages rise as labor demand increases
Wages are unaffected by product demand
Wages fall as the supply of workers increases
questions.answer: Wages rise as labor demand increases
questions.explanation: Higher product demand increases the marginal revenue product of labor, shifting labor demand right and raising equilibrium wages.

questions.question: Which of the following is an automatic stabilizer?
questions.explanation: Unemployment insurance automatically increases during recessions (as more people qualify) and decreases during expansions — without requiring new legislation.

questions.question: Globalization has generally led to which of the following outcomes?
options: The elimination of all trade barriers
questions.explanation: Globalization has increased international trade, investment, and economic interdependence, allowing countries to specialize based on comparative advantage.
